speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, interview with Sophie Waters. Highland Park, Illinois, USA. Sophie, you were talking about your husband's hospital stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: He walked by and he asked, is there a Dutch lady here? Is there a Dutch lady? And he got an answer. And this lady said, yes. And she mentioned her name, [INAUDIBLE], and my husband said, oh, then you must know us. My wife is a Kaiser.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, does she walk with a-- by my store with a little girl? And my husband, yes. Oh, she said. Then I know her well. So she said, yes, the Germans, they found-- we were going-- we were escaping, trying to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: They've killed my husband, and then they killed my son, and they knocked my head. They took-- she had diamonds hidden in her-- and she-- they had to take her head and put a new piece up there. She went through a terrible time. But she lived to be a very old lady. And I took her in. After the-- we were liberated, I took her in my house because I-- I got a big apartment from one of the Nazis, that was out in Amsterdam. We were on a train to Amsterdam. That's how we were-- after we were liberated, and then our house was not available.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to go back but it wasn't-- it was taken by others, of course. And then-- so I couldn't-- I was [? alterated. ?] Everything was taken. All my furniture was taken. They said that, as a gift from the Dutch people to the Germans. That's what they put on-- on the furniture. That's a-- a-- a loving gift from the Dutch people to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though all of your furniture and belongings were gone, could you have taken back your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know that. It wasn't there anymore. Nothing was there, you know? It was empty-- my home was empty. It was taken already, already a long time. After we were gone, everything was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was someone else living in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- in the-- in the house that belonged to my father, that's where the store was, above the store, we had living quarters. And there, it was a man, and my brother that was in the RAF, was in his uniform. And he went in and he said-- because people had taken us in for a short while, my husband, and myself, of course, my daughter. And they wanted to go on a vacation because it was already June, beginning of July. And they wanted to leave, because they-- that was a mixed marriage. They didn't go through what we had gone through, but they were very nice to take us in for the time being. And then my brother said to this man, you better get out of this store. This belongs to my father. And if you're not out by tomorrow, I'll just shoot you and you'll be behind the counter, dead. Well, he said, I'll be out. He was so afraid because my brother had the weapons, of course. And he went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the same man that had taken over your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no it was one of the people that was working for this man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was the boss then, you know? He-- the man fled already, so he took over. He was a tall man. And my brother was very short, but my brother was very-- had a good mouth, and that was very important. Show that you're not afraid, because if you show that, you're dead. And then he-- we went in. He got us mattresses. Of course, he had cigarettes, and from the army base, he had a lot of things. He had bread. He had everything. So he could bribe with that. And that's how we got mattresses that we could at least-- we had-- our building was-- had a couple of flights upstairs. So we could go in there and at least sleep, and then we bought food with whatever my brother gave us to start with. And that's how we started a new life, absolutely a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you decide to stay in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes, and we wanted to stay because there was-- we were making a nice living. But then we got a call from-- my sisters left already in '48 for America. And they called me at the end of '50, and they said, do you want to go through the same thing that you went through with your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter had just done three years in high school, and that's called there a gymnasium. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you want to go through that? I said, no way. I don't want to go through another-- be this way anymore. They said, well, then you better get your papers ready and come to America. And that's what we did. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1951, in April. We sailed off in April, 1951, and we came in on April 26th, and my daughter was just 15 the day we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you decided to come to the US, you spent six years back in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was life pretty much back to normal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, back to normal. Well, of course, you couldn't get everything that you had before, but with money, you could do a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your daughter seem after the experiences that she had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the experience with-- for her was that she had-- they put a school for children that had not been to school. They made a school special that she could go year round, and do second-- because she could read, already, when she was very little. And she'd done second, third, and fourth grade in this one year-- the first year. And then she went to a different school. She did the fifth and sixth grade. And in that school, the teacher said to me, Mrs. Waterman, of those children, you can have a dozen. She's the only one that really asks questions and will answer the right way. And I said, thank you very much but I only have one, and we'll just settle with that. But then when she went to fifth grade, she wasn't as far with her mathematics as the children in that school and there was no-- it was only a half-day school because there was no coal to heat the school with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a gas heater in the apartment that I had gotten, and I said, well, can you give her-- tutor her, and I will pay for that? It was very little money, but I would like you to have more children, because after all, my daughter was a young-- and he was a young man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, sure. And he took another couple of children and used my apartment for tutoring her. And then I got a beautiful letter from him. After a few months, he wrote, I must say, your daughter has caught up with everything the same as my other pupils, and she does not need to be tutored anymore. And that was very kind, because money was very important for a young man like that. He was going to get married. And I know my daughter bought him a nice gift for his marriage, and that's how-- and then she went to sixth grade. And then from there she went to the gymnasium. And she was all of 12 years old when she went to high school. Actually, gymnasium is even better than high school. And-- and she came through. After first grade-- to go through first grade gymnasium, you have to be very, very good. Otherwise, they do not let you go to the second grade. They just say, out. And then she went to second grade, and then she went to third grade. And then she couldn't finish that, because in April, we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a sponsor, friends of mine that we had met in camp, Jewish people. And I became very friendly with them, and we had sent money over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was not allowed. We gave, like, say, $1,000 in Dutch money to a man that said, I will see that there's $1,000 in America for you. And we let that, for these people that were-- we thought were very nice. They were nice people. We could trust them. And that's how we got a few thousand dollars here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then, of course, we had money for our trip. And then the IRS, of course, came and they were not easy. And I still have the papers how much I had to pay before I was allowed to leave Holland. They took, really, the shirts off our back. But we thought we're going to start a new life in a free country and that was worth everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we came to Chicago, because my sisters-- two sisters-- were living already in Chicago. And they had a very big rented place. And they had so much space that they could put five of us. My parents came with me at the time, and we were three people. Five of us up in their house. We came by train from New York. We came-- first when we arrived, we went into the Stettler Hotel, because that was right there, and we had to see these people that had our money. And then we came-- that Sunday we came to Chicago, and my one sister had made a beautiful spread for lunch. We came in. We thought it was a feast. She had everything on the table you can imagine. And then they put us up for a few weeks. And then we went shopping for an apartment. And everything was only allowed to charge that much with the rental-- price on the rent. I don't know how that was called. And there was a Greek, and he had a big apartment, and we could use that because there was a bedroom for my parents, a bedroom for us, and a bedroom for my daughter, and a living room. And we had a van come from Holland with us, on the boat. We came with the New Amsterdam. And I had brought some rugs with me, and my sofa, and mementos, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who in your family survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all in the same camp

speaker: interviewer
text: And everyone in your family survived? Parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Except my-- except-- my younger sister was married just a few months. They took her husband from her arm in Amsterdam, and sent him to Mauthausen. And after a couple of months, they were sent a note, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the flight, we killed him. He tried to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was, of course,a big lie, because he had written a letter to my sister. Give my love to that one and that one. That was the baker and the butcher. So in other words, he had nothing to eat, you know? And my other brother, my sister's husband, was in Bergen-Belsen, and he died in January 1945. He died-- But both your sisters survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- all my family survived. We were the only-- I can really say the only family with six children that came through the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you adjusted to your life here in the US, did you used to spend a lot of time thinking about what had happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I always said, don't let's talk about it all the time because that's not good for your-- for your attitude. You-- you-- it's-- it's such a bad experience that it is not good, and nobody wants to hear bad things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your daughter ever talk about her experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. She-- never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that that affected her?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was much better for her not to talk about it than keep going on. But I always said, you can tell your children. She said, Mommy, you put it on paper. And I did. I wrote six pages, at least. And I wrote to my children and grandchildren, this is what happened to your grandparents. And I wrote it all with the dates and everything. And she has it, and she was going to put it on tape for me. So I know that she was very interested in the history of our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family all adjust to life in the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband was most difficult, because he went to work in a cleaning store-- you know, big cleaning. His English-- he had to go to school to learn English, and he went to night school. He learned very fast. And then he saw people throwing out whole loaves of bread on the garbage. And he came back, he said, Sophie this is not a country for us. They throw away things we would have been happy to have in camp. He mention-- he said, I think we should go back to Holland, because this is bad. People are spoiled rotten here. He always said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for a living here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here-- then he went and applied-- he learned-- my sisters had the drapery business. So my one sister taught him a little about it, and he went to the [? boss's ?] store in Chicago that was at that time. And he applied. And by the time he got home-- my dad was home-- my dad said, they called already. They want you to come in to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he worked for them. And I had the papers. He made-- he brought two silver dollars home and a bunch of flowers and a big write up in their paper that he was the best salesman they had. And don't forget, he had a big accent and everything. But they had put someone-- at that time, they had people going around to see how people respond. So he said to me, you know, I think I made a good day today. I said, you did? He said, yeah, the lady she needed for the whole house. I sold her everything under the sun. I said, well, thank God, you're now not in the red anymore. You're in the black. He said, I certainly am. Then we found out it was for someone that was just looking, you know? Then they found out that he had done his best to sell. Did you also work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked right away. I came in on Sunday to Chicago, and Monday night, I had a job. I applied for a job, and the gentleman was Mr. Gately. He was the head of the Park District. He had a big store in South Side of Chicago. And I spoke to Mr. Gately, and he said, you seem like a lady that just came from New York. You're so well-dressed. I had all-- everything custom made in Holland. And I said, yes, that's right. I did come from New York, but I come from Holland originally. And I showed him my passport. He couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, in which department do you want to work? I said, well, I know everything about leather. I think I'll go in the leather department. He says, OK, you'll make $30 a week. Well, I didn't know how the money was and I know in '51 you could do a lot with $30. So I found that out later. So then there was a young lady and she was in my sisters' store. And she says, oh, in the dress shop opposite, they're asking for a salesperson. I said, what do I know about dresses? I've never sold a dress in my life. And she said, well, go in there and talk. So I spoke there for five minutes and Mr. Leonard, the owner, said, the job is yours. You'll make $35. Well, in those years, $5 was a lot of money. And I said, when can I start? Because I first have to get my daughter into school. And my sister came and said, don't worry about your daughter. I'll get her in school. You work. You wanted to work? Work. And you enjoyed your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoy-- I stayed with him for three years. And he was-- he loved me like a child loves-- yes. I saw him a few years ago in California. I looked him up, and he picked me up and kissed me and he said, Sophie, you made my store what it was. And that was quite a compliment, because he had such cheap ware, and it was such a beautiful store, that I said, please get me better merchandise, because that I can sell. And, of course, these people-- the woman that was there, the manager before me-- said the foreigners always think they know everything better. It was true. And I said no, I'm not saying that I know better, but I know that I can sell better merchandise than what we sell. And Mr. Leonard will make more money on it. And indeed, that was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was true. Now, tell me, it's 50 years since you were liberated with your family. Is there any last message that you'd like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what else can I say? I was never actually mistreated. The only way I know was a man came into my-- when we had the store, and we used to sell these things that go around the leather that they put around when it's bad weather. We call them [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know what they call that here. And he said, I would like a pair of [INAUDIBLE]. I said, well, I will see if I have your size, because they were different heights and different widths. So he took his lapel and showed me the inside that he had the Nazi emblem.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, that makes no difference to me. I-- that's how I was. I was young at that time. And I said, because everybody's money is the same. If I have them, if it's your money, or someone else's, you have to pay for them like every-- anybody else. And if I have them, they're yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came-- I went to the upstairs. We had the goods upstairs. And I brought down a couple of pairs. I said, try them on and see which one fits. And he thanked me and paid for them. That was a bad-- I was trembling after he left, because that was a very bad experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was in-- that was '43, it must have been, at least, yes. In '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what is most important to you today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most important is that I lost my husband 18 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter? You have one daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandchild-- and great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And great-grandchildren. How many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have five grandchildren, and I have four great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they give you a lot of happiness, don't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are all lovely, lovely. They give me a lot of happiness. And I always say God has blessed me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Sophie.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken from-- this is myself, my husband, and my daughter, was taken in December, 1940. We were going to a wedding of one of my sisters, and that was taken at--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter. I think she was, at that time, three years old. This was taken in Amsterdam, Holland at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Rodi. The name was taken from my mother-- my mother-in-law was [? Rhosa, ?] and my mother was Dinah. And we put the two names together, and that's how we got to Rodi.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and we're-- we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my daughter when we were liberated in France in 1944. We were liberated by the American unit. I don't remember the unit, but I know they were some very wonderful people. And she was marching there on the square, with others, of course, with the-- it was a beautiful idea that we were liberated, the idea that we were not in hands of the Germans anymore. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these are my-- my mum and my dad. And that-- this picture shows that they were married 50 years, thank God. And they are both deceased and buried in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken on my grandson's bar mitzvah 22 years ago. My husband-- he deceased 18 years ago. My sister's living in Holland still. She's a widow.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1939, the wedding of my brother of my late husband. And the whole family is there-- was there. And now there are three people still alive, on the whole paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my daughter, husband, grandchildren, and two great-grandchildren. This was taken in April, 1995, to make it short. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are my great-grandchildren. Danielle is four, and Blake is going to be two next week.
